revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Kuša]][[de:Kusha-shū]][[en:Kusha-shū]][[fr:Kusha (bouddhisme)]][[ja:倶舎宗]][[no:Kusha-shū]][[ru:Куся]][[uk:Куся]][[vi:Câu-xá tông]][[zh_yue:俱舍宗]][[zh:俱舍宗]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1782479]]
Wikidata name: Kusha-shū
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|School of Buddhism}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}}
{{Buddhism}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kusha-shū'''|{{linktext|倶|舎|宗}}}} was one of the six schools of  Buddhism introduced to Japan during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]s.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}} Along with the {{nihongo|[[:en:Tattvasiddhi|Tattvasiddhi]] school| |Jōjitsu-shū}} and the [[Risshū (Buddhism)|Risshū]], it is a school of [[Nikaya Buddhism]], which is sometimes derisively known to [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhism]] as "the [[:en:Hinayana|Hinayana]]".

A [[Sarvastivada]] school, Kusha-shū focused on [[:en:Abhidharma|Abhidharma]] analysis based on the "Commentary on the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Abhidharma-kosa|Abhidharmakośabhaṣya]]''|倶舎論}}" by the fourth-century [[:en:Gandhara|Gandhara]]n philosopher [[:en:Vasubandhu|Vasubandhu]]. The school takes its name from that authoritative text.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}}

Names commonly associated with the Kusha-shū are [[:en:Dōshō|Dōshō]] (道昭 638–700), Joe (644–714), Chitsū (智通 ?–?), Chitatsu (智達 ?–?), and [[:en:Genbō|Genbō]] (玄昉 ?–746).

==See also==
*[[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]

==References==
{{Reflist}}

===Bibliography===
*  {{cite book|last1=Buswell|first1=Robert|authorlink1=Robert Buswell Jr.|last2=Lopez|first2=Donald S.|authorlink2=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-fWKngEACAAJ|year=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-15786-3}}

{{Buddhism-stub}}
{{Japan-reli-stub}}

[[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]]

Names commonly associated with the Kusha-shū are [[:en:Dōshō|Dōshō]] (道昭 638–700), Joe (644–714), Chitsū (智通 ?–?), Chitatsu (智達 ?–?), and [[:en:Genbō|Genbō]] (玄昉 ?–746).

A [[Sarvastivada]] school, Kusha-shū focused on [[:en:Abhidharma|Abhidharma]] analysis based on the "Commentary on the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Abhidharma-kosa|Abhidharmakośabhaṣya]]''|倶舎論}}" by the fourth-century [[:en:Gandhara|Gandhara]]n philosopher [[:en:Vasubandhu|Vasubandhu]]. The school takes its name from that authoritative text.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}}

Names commonly associated with the Kusha-shū are [[:en:Dōshō|Dōshō]] (道昭 638–700), Joe (644–714), Chitsū (智通 ?–?), Chitatsu (智達 ?–?), and [[:en:Genbō|Genbō]] (玄昉 ?–746).

*[[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Kuša]][[de:Kusha-shū]][[en:Kusha-shū]][[fr:Kusha (bouddhisme)]][[ja:倶舎宗]][[no:Kusha-shū]][[ru:Куся]][[uk:Куся]][[vi:Câu-xá tông]][[zh-yue:俱舍宗]][[zh:俱舍宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Kuša]][[de:Kusha-shū]][[en:Kusha-shū]][[fr:Kusha (bouddhisme)]][[ja:倶舎宗]][[no:Kusha-shū]][[ru:Куся]][[uk:Куся]][[vi:Câu-xá tông]][[zh-yue:俱舍宗]][[zh:俱舍宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Kuša]][[de:Kusha-shū]][[en:Kusha-shū]][[fr:Kusha (bouddhisme)]][[ja:倶舎宗]][[no:Kusha-shū]][[ru:Куся]][[uk:Куся]][[vi:Câu-xá tông]][[zh-yue:俱舍宗]][[zh:俱舍宗]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1782479]] Wikidata name: Kusha-shū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|School of Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} {{Buddhism}} The {{nihongo|'''Kusha-shū'''|{{linktext|倶|舎|宗}}}} was one of the six schools of Buddhism introduced to Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka}} and {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}s.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}} Along with the {{nihongo|[[:en:Tattvasiddhi|Tattvasiddhi]] school| |Jōjitsu-shū}} and the [[Risshū (Buddhism)|Risshū]], it is a school of [[Nikaya Buddhism]], which is sometimes derisively known to {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana Buddhism}} as "the {{ill|Hinayana|en|Hinayana|WD=Q193814|lt=Hinayana}}".
A [[Sarvastivada]] school, Kusha-shū focused on {{ill|Abhidharma|en|Abhidharma|WD=Q612405|lt=Abhidharma}} analysis based on the "Commentary on the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Abhidharma-kosa|Abhidharmakośabhaṣya]]''|倶舎論}}" by the fourth-century {{ill|Gandhara|en|Gandhara|WD=Q213651|lt=Gandhara}}n philosopher {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu}}. The school takes its name from that authoritative text.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}}
Names commonly associated with the Kusha-shū are {{ill|Dōshō|en|Dōshō|WD=Q724005|lt=Dōshō}} (道昭 638–700), Joe (644–714), Chitsū (智通 ?–?), Chitatsu (智達 ?–?), and {{ill|Genbō|en|Genbō|WD=Q657737|lt=Genbō}} (玄昉 ?–746).
==See also== *{{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
===Bibliography=== * {{cite book|last1=Buswell|first1=Robert|authorlink1=Robert Buswell Jr.|last2=Lopez|first2=Donald S.|authorlink2=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-fWKngEACAAJ|year=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-15786-3}}

The school takes its name from that authoritative text.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Kuša]][[de:Kusha-shū]][[en:Kusha-shū]][[fr:Kusha (bouddhisme)]][[ja:倶舎宗]][[no:Kusha-shū]][[ru:Куся]][[uk:Куся]][[vi:Câu-xá tông]][[zh-yue:俱舍宗]][[zh:俱舍宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Kuša]][[de:Kusha-shū]][[en:Kusha-shū]][[fr:Kusha (bouddhisme)]][[ja:倶舎宗]][[no:Kusha-shū]][[ru:Куся]][[uk:Куся]][[vi:Câu-xá tông]][[zh-yue:俱舍宗]][[zh:俱舍宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cs:Kuša]][[de:Kusha-shū]][[en:Kusha-shū]][[fr:Kusha (bouddhisme)]][[ja:倶舎宗]][[no:Kusha-shū]][[ru:Куся]][[uk:Куся]][[vi:Câu-xá tông]][[zh-yue:俱舍宗]][[zh:俱舍宗]]
{{Short description|School of Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} {{Buddhism}} The {{nihongo|'''Kusha-shū'''|{{linktext|倶|舎|宗}}}} was one of the six schools of Buddhism introduced to Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka}} and {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}s.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}} Along with the {{nihongo|[[:en:Tattvasiddhi|Tattvasiddhi]] school| |Jōjitsu-shū}} and the [[Risshū (Buddhism)|Risshū]], it is a school of [[Nikaya Buddhism]], which is sometimes derisively known to {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana Buddhism}} as "the {{ill|Hinayana|en|Hinayana|WD=Q193814|lt=Hinayana}}".
A [[Sarvastivada]] school, Kusha-shū focused on {{ill|Abhidharma|en|Abhidharma|WD=Q612405|lt=Abhidharma}} analysis based on the "Commentary on the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Abhidharma-kosa|Abhidharmakośabhaṣya]]''|倶舎論}}" by the fourth-century {{ill|Gandhara|en|Gandhara|WD=Q213651|lt=Gandhara}}n philosopher {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu}}. The school takes its name from that authoritative text.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}}
Names commonly associated with the Kusha-shū are {{ill|Dōshō|en|Dōshō|WD=Q724005|lt=Dōshō}} (道昭 638–700), Joe (644–714), Chitsū (智通 ?–?), Chitatsu (智達 ?–?), and {{ill|Genbō|en|Genbō|WD=Q657737|lt=Genbō}} (玄昉 ?–746).
==See also== *{{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}}

{{Short description|School of Buddhism}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}}
{{Buddhism}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kusha-shū'''|{{linktext|倶|舎|宗}}}} was one of the six schools of  Buddhism introduced to Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka period}} and {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}s.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}} Along with the {{nihongo|[[:en:Tattvasiddhi|Tattvasiddhi]] school| |Jōjitsu-shū}} and the [[Risshū (Buddhism)|Risshū]], it is a school of [[Nikaya Buddhism]], which is sometimes derisively known to {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana Buddhism|1=Mahayana}} as "the {{ill|Hinayana|en|Hinayana|WD=Q193814|lt=Hinayana|1=Hinayana}}".

A [[Sarvastivada]] school, Kusha-shū focused on {{ill|Abhidharma|en|Abhidharma|WD=Q612405|lt=Abhidharma|1=Abhidharma}} analysis based on the "Commentary on the {{nihongo|''[[:en:Abhidharma-kosa|Abhidharmakośabhaṣya]]''|倶舎論}}" by the fourth-century {{ill|Gandhara|en|Gandhara|WD=Q213651|lt=Gandhara|1=Gandhara}}n philosopher {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu|1=Vasubandhu}}. The school takes its name from that authoritative text.{{sfn|Buswell|Lopez|2013|p=574}}

Names commonly associated with the Kusha-shū are {{ill|Dōshō|en|Dōshō|WD=Q724005|lt=Dōshō|1=Dōshō}} (道昭 638–700), Joe (644–714), Chitsū (智通 ?–?), Chitatsu (智達 ?–?), and {{ill|Genbō|en|Genbō|WD=Q657737|lt=Genbō|1=Genbō}} (玄昉 ?–746).
